# Template: Demo Trainer Guide
# Purpose: Step-by-step presenter guide for Google Workspace / GCP demo delivery
# Generated by: demo-engineer agent
# Used by: Sales engineer, CE, or trainer delivering the demo

---

# Demo Trainer Guide

**Demo name:** {demo_name}
**Customer:** {company_name}
**Audience:** {personas — e.g., "CTO, 3 infrastructure architects, IT Director"}
**Date:** {date}
**Duration:** {total_duration} minutes
**Presenter:** {presenter_name}
**Technical support:** {support_name} (on standby for technical issues)

---

## Pre-Demo Checklist

### T-5 Business Days

- [ ] Confirm demo environment is provisioned — run `orchestrator.gs` against `{customerKey}`
- [ ] Verify all Gmail labels and email threads are present (Scenario 1–10 check)
- [ ] Verify all Google Docs are accessible from demo account
- [ ] Verify all Google Sheets open and display correctly (charts rendered)
- [ ] Verify all Calendar events appear correctly with correct dates
- [ ] Verify Google Chat spaces exist with seeded messages
- [ ] Test all Google Meet links — confirm they create rooms
- [ ] Test Gemini features in demo account: Gmail drafting, Docs assist, Meet summaries
- [ ] Run cleanup + re-provision at least once to confirm repeatability
- [ ] Confirm customer-specific content: {company} name correct throughout, domain consistent
- [ ] Review customer research brief — load top 3 pain points into memory

### T-2 Business Days

- [ ] Dry run full demo end-to-end — time each section
- [ ] Confirm all browser bookmarks set (see Bookmark Setup below)
- [ ] Prepare fallback screenshots for each demo section (in case of connectivity issues)
- [ ] Brief support person on their role — when to step in, what not to say
- [ ] Confirm room setup if hybrid: screen sharing, second monitor, clicker
- [ ] Print physical backup of this guide (1 copy — use only if laptop fails)

### T-1 Day

- [ ] Change all email/doc dates to feel current (use find/replace or re-run generator)
- [ ] Sign in to demo Google account — confirm no personal accounts visible
- [ ] Clear browser history and autocomplete (don't leak other customer names)
- [ ] Set screen resolution to 1920×1080 or higher
- [ ] Set display zoom to 125% for large screens (adjust based on room)
- [ ] Close all unnecessary tabs and applications
- [ ] Turn off all notifications (OS-level + browser)
- [ ] Charge laptop fully; bring charging cable

### Day-of (T-30 minutes)

- [ ] Open browser with pre-configured demo profile
- [ ] Open all tabs in correct order (see Tab Order below)
- [ ] Test screen sharing before audience arrives
- [ ] Test audio and video if virtual
- [ ] Confirm Google Meet room is working
- [ ] Quick Gemini test: draft one email in Gmail — confirm feature is available
- [ ] Water on the table

### Tab Order for Demo

1. **Gmail** — inbox view — mail.google.com
2. **Gmail** — Project Kickoff thread open
3. **Google Drive** — demo company folder
4. **Google Docs** — Project Charter open
5. **Google Sheets** — KPI Dashboard open (on Summary tab)
6. **Google Calendar** — week view showing current demo week
7. **Google Chat** — proj-{project} space open
8. **Google Meet** — meet.google.com (new meeting)
9. **Google Admin Console** — admin.google.com (if Admin demo section included)
10. **Looker Studio** — dashboard link (if data analytics section included)

---

## Opening Script

> Deliver this verbatim for the first 90 seconds, then adapt to the conversation.

**[Look at the room, not the screen]**

"Thank you all for making time today.

Before I get into any slides or demos, I want to take 60 seconds to set the context for why we're here — because this session is going to look different from a typical vendor demo.

We've done our homework on {company}. We know you're [reference 1–2 specific findings from research brief]. So instead of showing you generic features, everything you're going to see today is set up to look like {company}'s world.

My ask: stop me any time. If something doesn't connect to your reality, tell me. If you want to go deeper on something, tell me. I'd rather have a conversation than a presentation.

We have [X] minutes together. Here's how I'm planning to use them. [Gesture to slide or verbal overview.] Does that work for you, or is there something you'd like to adjust?"

**[Pause — let them respond. Adapt the agenda if they redirect.]**

---

## Section Timing Table

| # | Section | Duration | Key Message | Gemini Feature Shown |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Gmail — everyday communication | 8 min | Intelligent inbox, reduce email noise | Gemini drafting, Smart Reply, Summary |
| 2 | Gmail — security workflow | 5 min | Security embedded in communication | Labels, policy-enforced retention |
| 3 | Google Docs — collaboration | 8 min | Real-time co-authoring, no versioning chaos | Gemini writing assist, suggestions mode |
| 4 | Google Sheets — data + insights | 8 min | Data where decisions happen | Gemini formula generation, chart creation |
| 5 | Google Calendar — time management | 5 min | Calendar that works for the organization | Smart scheduling, Meet integration |
| 6 | Google Chat — team coordination | 6 min | One place for project work | Spaces, threads, file sharing |
| 7 | Google Meet — modern meetings | 5 min | Meetings that produce outcomes | Transcription, summary, Gemini in Meet |
| 8 | Google Admin — IT control | 5 min | IT admin without the overhead | User management, policy, security |
| 9 | Q&A | 10 min | — | — |
| | **Total** | **60 min** | | |

**Buffer rule:** If running long after section 4, skip sections 8 and compress 5–6. Never cut Q&A below 8 minutes.

---

## Say This / Show That

### Section 1: Gmail

| Say This | Show That |
|----------|-----------|
| "Let's start where most people spend their day — email. But email that actually helps you." | Open Gmail inbox — show clean, organized inbox with labels visible |
| "You can see here how {company}'s email is organized — there are project threads, security notifications, finance approvals — everything has a home." | Hover over label hierarchy |
| "The first thing I want to show you is how Gemini can help your team write better emails faster." | Click compose — show Gemini drafting button — type "write a follow-up to our security audit meeting asking for the remediation plan" |
| "In about 10 seconds, that's a professional draft ready to review. Your team spends less time writing, more time deciding." | Show generated draft — highlight quality |
| "Now here's a capability that saves a lot of time for managers and executives — summarizing long email threads." | Open the Project Kickoff thread (5+ messages) |
| "Gemini reads the whole thread and surfaces: what was decided, what needs action, who's doing what." | Click Summarize — show summary panel |
| "One more thing — smart labels and retention. This thread is automatically tagged as Projects/{company} DT Initiative." | Show label — mention admin policy drives this |

**Transition:** "Gmail handles communication. Let me show you what happens when communication needs to turn into a document."

---

### Section 2: Gmail — Security Workflow

| Say This | Show That |
|----------|-----------|
| "One use case that resonates with CISOs and IT teams — security-critical communications." | Navigate to Security/Audits label |
| "Here's how a security audit finding flows through the organization. The team gets an alert, acknowledges it, tracks remediation — all in a documented, auditable thread." | Open the security audit email thread — scroll through |
| "What you're seeing here — every email, every action, every response — is logged, labeled, and retained according to your policy. When your auditor asks for evidence, you click here." | Point to retention indicator |
| "And crucially — this is not a shadow IT communication. It's under your IT governance." | — |

**Transition:** "Now let me show you where that work actually gets done — in Google Docs."

---

### Section 3: Google Docs

| Say This | Show That |
|----------|-----------|
| "This is the Project Charter for {company}'s transformation initiative." | Open Project Charter doc |
| "The first thing you notice — three people are in this document right now, in real time." | Point to collaborator cursors/avatars |
| "There are no versions called Charter_FINAL_v3_Anna_revised.docx. There is one document. The history is in here." | Click File > Version history — show timeline |
| "Your team can suggest changes without overwriting the original." | Switch to Suggestion mode — make a small edit |
| "And if {CTO name} wants to leave context for a decision, they comment inline — the comment threads show the thinking behind the document." | Show comment thread if present |
| "Now here's Gemini in Docs. Let's say I want to add an executive summary to this charter." | Click Gemini icon — type "write a 3-sentence executive summary for this project charter" |
| "It reads the entire document and writes a summary in context. I can insert it directly." | Show result — insert |

**Transition:** "Documents are great for narrative. When you need structure and numbers, let me show you Sheets."

---

### Section 4: Google Sheets

| Say This | Show That |
|----------|-----------|
| "This is your IT KPI Dashboard. Everything in one place — reliability, performance, cost, security posture." | Open KPI Dashboard on Summary tab |
| "The green, amber, red you see — this isn't manually set. It's formula-driven. The data updates, the status updates." | Point to conditional formatting — show a cell formula |
| "Here's something your finance team will use every month: cloud cost tracking." | Switch to Cloud tab in budget spreadsheet |
| "Now, Gemini in Sheets. Let's say I need a formula I don't know." | Click an empty cell — type @ or use Gemini chip |
| "I'll ask in plain English: 'formula to calculate the percentage of budget spent and turn the cell red if it's above 90%.'" | Type prompt — show formula generated |
| "That formula would have taken me 5 minutes to figure out. 10 seconds." | Insert formula — show it working |

**Transition:** "Data and documents live in Drive. Let me show you how the team coordinates around both — in Calendar and Chat."

---

### Section 5: Google Calendar

| Say This | Show That |
|----------|-----------|
| "Here's a week in the life of your IT team at {company}." | Open Calendar — week view showing seeded events |
| "You can see the project standup, the 1:1s, the external customer review — and notice the color coding. That's policy-driven." | Point to color coding |
| "Every meeting has a Google Meet link — already generated, nobody has to 'start the meeting' or 'find the link.'" | Click on one event — show Meet link in description |
| "The description isn't blank — it has the agenda, the pre-reading, the links to the relevant document." | Scroll through event description |
| "When your team uses Calendar this way, meetings start on time and end with decisions." | — |

**Transition:** "The running coordination happens in Chat."

---

### Section 6: Google Chat

| Say This | Show That |
|----------|-----------|
| "This is the {company} project space for the Digital Transformation Initiative." | Open proj-{project} space |
| "Notice the structure — updates are threaded, not a wall of messages. I can see what was discussed, what was decided, and who owns what." | Scroll through seeded messages — point to a thread |
| "Files shared in Chat land in Drive — they're not in some separate archive." | Point to attachment in a message |
| "And here's the security ops space — this is where incidents happen in real time." | Switch to security-ops space |
| "When that automated alert came in at 3am, the on-call engineer responded in 7 minutes. The whole investigation is right here, documented." | Show incident thread |

**Transition:** "Let me show you the meeting experience."

---

### Section 7: Google Meet

| Say This | Show That |
|----------|-----------|
| "Google Meet has three capabilities that change how meetings work." | Open meet.google.com — start a meeting |
| "First — transcription. This happens automatically. You don't have to take notes." | Show transcription panel (or simulate with a recording clip) |
| "Second — when the meeting ends, Gemini produces a summary: key topics, decisions, action items." | Show a meeting summary artifact |
| "Third — Meet works on any device, with no software to install for external guests." | Point to join options |
| "One more thing for large organizations: breakout rooms, Q&A, polls, whiteboards — all native." | Show host controls briefly |

**Transition:** "Behind all of this is the admin layer — this is what your IT team sees."

---

### Section 8: Google Admin Console

| Say This | Show That |
|----------|-----------|
| "This is the Google Admin Console — the single pane of glass for your IT team." | Open admin.google.com |
| "From here: users, groups, devices, security, apps, billing — everything." | Quick navigation tour |
| "User provisioning: new employee starts Monday. IT creates the account here — email, Drive, Chat, Calendar, all of it is ready." | Click Users — Add user (stop before creating) |
| "Security dashboard: two-step verification adoption, suspicious sign-ins, active sessions." | Click Security > Overview |
| "If a device is lost: one click, the device is wiped remotely. The data never left Google's infrastructure." | Point to Device management briefly |
| "Every admin action is logged. When your auditor asks who changed what policy and when — it's in the audit log." | Click Admin audit log briefly |

---

## Transition Phrases

Use these to maintain flow between sections:

| From | To | Phrase |
|------|----|--------|
| Gmail | Docs | "Communication needs to turn into documents. Let me show you where the work lives." |
| Docs | Sheets | "When narrative needs to become structure and numbers, this is where it goes." |
| Sheets | Calendar | "Data and documents are how you store decisions. Calendar is how you make them." |
| Calendar | Chat | "Meetings get scheduled. The work in between happens here." |
| Chat | Meet | "Sometimes text isn't enough. This is what a meeting looks like." |
| Meet | Admin | "That's the experience for users. Let me show you what IT sees behind it." |
| Any section | Q&A | "I want to make sure we save time for your questions — let me stop presenting and hear from you." |

---

## Objection Handling (Inline)

Objections you will encounter — handle in the moment, don't defer:

**"We're fully committed to Microsoft 365."**
Response: "That's common — and we work with many companies in exactly that position. A few questions: when does your current agreement renew? And are there specific capabilities — like AI or data analytics — where you'd want to evaluate whether Microsoft is still the best fit? We're not proposing you rip and replace — but the contract renewal is often the right moment to make sure you've made an active choice."

**"Gemini / AI features aren't important to us right now."**
Response: "That's fair — most of our customers started in the same place. The question I'd ask is: your competitors are adopting these tools. The question isn't whether AI becomes important, it's whether you want to build the capability before or after it becomes urgent. The formula generation I showed earlier alone — 10 seconds vs. 5 minutes — that compounds across 500 people."

**"Our developers won't switch tools."**
Response: "Google doesn't require a switch — we integrate with VS Code, JetBrains, GitHub. Gemini Code Assist works where your developers already are. The ones who try it usually become advocates."

**"What happens to our data?"**
Response: "Great question — and I want to be specific, not general. Your data stays in {europe-west1 / specified region}. Google does not train models on your data. The Data Processing Agreement is available and we can walk through it with your legal team. Would it help to schedule a session specifically on data governance and compliance?"

**"We tried Google Workspace before and our users hated it."**
Response: "Can I ask when that was? And what specifically didn't work? The product has changed significantly in the last 3 years — Gemini features, Meet improvements, Chat for team coordination. I'd want to understand the specific friction before assuming the answer is the same today."

---

## Closing Script

> Deliver this in the final 5 minutes.

**[Stop sharing screen. Face the audience.]**

"Before I ask for your reaction — let me summarize what you've seen and what it means for {company}.

You saw email intelligence that saves your team time on drafting, reading, and triaging. You saw documents where your team collaborates in real time — no version chaos. You saw data and dashboards that update themselves. You saw meetings that produce artifacts without anyone taking notes. And you saw IT admin that gives your team control without complexity.

But more than features — you saw how these tools connect. One platform, one identity, one place for the team's work.

Now the honest question: of what you saw today, what resonated most? And what questions do you still have?"

**[Pause. Let them answer. Take notes.]**

"Thank you. Based on what you've told me — {reflect 1–2 things they said}. Here's what I'd suggest as a natural next step:

[Choose based on their response:]
- If technically engaged: "A hands-on session where your team tries the environment themselves."
- If commercially engaged: "A business case workshop where we quantify the value for {company} specifically."
- If skeptical: "A limited proof of concept — your team, your data, 30 days."

What would be most useful?"

---

## Post-Demo Follow-Up Email Template

Send within 4 hours of the meeting:

**Subject:** Thank you — {demo_name} follow-up + next steps

```
Hi {contact_name},

Thank you for the time today — I found our conversation genuinely useful.

A few things from the session:

What I heard resonated most: {1–2 specific things they said — from your notes}

Promised follow-ups:
• {Item 1 — e.g., "Reference customer intro — I'll reach out by {date + 2 days}"}
• {Item 2 — e.g., "The security compliance document — attached"}
• {Item 3 — e.g., "Proposed POC scope — I'll draft and share by {date + 3 days}"}

Suggested next step: {Specific proposal based on their closing response}

I'll follow up on {date + 2 days} to see if the above is useful and to propose a date.

Best,
{presenter_name}
{title}, Google Cloud
{phone}
```

---

## Emergency Fallback Procedures

**If internet connection fails:**
- Switch to hotspot immediately — have phone ready with hotspot configured
- If no connectivity: use pre-loaded screenshots in folder: `~/Desktop/Demo-Fallback/{customerKey}`
- Narrate the screenshots: "What you would normally see here is..." — keep the story alive

**If Gemini features are unavailable:**
- Skip to the next section; do not dwell
- In Q&A: "We had a feature unavailability today — I'd suggest we schedule a follow-up hands-on session where your team can try it directly."

**If a demo account is missing content:**
- Ask for a 2-minute break — use your phone to check if orchestrator.gs log shows an error
- If not recoverable: explain the platform and narrate what the customer would see
- Never make up or fake a demo by typing fake content live — it backfires

**If the customer asks a question you can't answer:**
- "That's a good question — I don't want to give you a wrong answer. Let me get back to you within 24 hours with the accurate information." Then write it down visibly.
- Never guess on compliance, legal, or contractual questions.
